preFix.title: Siting and setting up a rabbit hutch and run
preFix.subtitle: Drainage, shade, predator-proofing, and the daily routine that keeps a colony healthy.
preFix.excerpt: Where you put a rabbit hutch matters more than what it looks like. Poor drainage, direct afternoon sun, and thin wire mesh on the floor are the three most common causes of preventable losses in a first colony. A guide to siting, setting up, and securing a rabbit hutch and run for meat or fibre production.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Pork / Rabbit section, open-access factsheets. University of Wisconsin Extension, rabbit production guides (UW-Extension publication). Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), rabbit chapter, Project Gutenberg. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits, Crown copyright.

content.text: Rabbit housing fails or succeeds before the first animal arrives. The three things that matter most are drainage underfoot, shade from afternoon sun, and wire gauge strong enough to stop a fox. Get those right and a basic setup will serve for years.
Choosing the site

Rabbits need shelter from driving rain and from heat. In a UK summer, a hutch facing south or south-west in full sun will overheat by early afternoon and rabbits have no sweat glands to cope with it. Face the hutch north or north-east, or place it where a wall, hedge, or building throws shade from midday onward.

Ground drainage determines how much cleaning work the setup creates. A concrete pad or compacted hardcore base lets urine drain away and makes scrubbing easier. Wet soil under a hutch sours quickly and raises humidity inside. Raise timber legs on concrete feet or bricks to prevent rotting.

Keep the site at least 2 metres from the boundary if foxes work that line regularly. A gap between the run and the fence gives you space to check the wire and stops foxes reaching through from outside.
The hutch and run dimensions
For a breeding
doe
and her litter, the sleeping compartment should be at least 60 cm deep, 60 cm wide, and 45 cm high. A single grow-out
colony run

for 4 to 6 rabbits needs a minimum of 2 m by 1 m of floor space, with a covered section for shelter. Cramped housing causes fighting and disease. Allow more space than you think you need when building.

attrs.body: Use 19-gauge (1 mm) welded mesh, maximum 25 mm aperture. A fox can reach animals through larger gaps. Check the gauge on the label.
attrs.title: Floor wire gauge

content.text: Predator-proofing

Foxes, mink, rats, and stoats all prey on rabbits. The wire must be stapled or clipped flush to the frame with no gaps at corners. Check every join when new before you put animals in. A fox will work along the wire testing each fixing; one loose staple is enough.

Lay an apron of flat wire mesh on the ground around a colony run to stop digging. Pin it with tent pegs and let grass grow through it. Rats will tunnel under a run that sits on bare soil without an apron. If the run is raised on legs, check underneath regularly for signs of digging.

A single strand of electric fencing at fox-nose height, around 20 cm off the ground, placed outside the wire is the most reliable deterrent for persistent foxes. Use a battery or mains energiser rated for a short perimeter. Check it weekly with a fence tester.
Setting up inside the hutch

Line the sleeping area with a 5 cm layer of straw. Wood shavings are fine for the run floor but straw insulates better in the sleeping compartment. Hay serves as both bedding and food; change the sleeping area straw every 4 to 5 days and spot-clean the run floor daily.

Water bottles clip to the wire at lip height. Check the spout each morning as the ball-valve nipple can block with scale, and animals dehydrate quickly, especially in summer. A gravity drinker is a backup but buckets get fouled with bedding rapidly.
A nest box for a breeding
doe
should be a wooden box, around 35 cm wide by 25 cm deep, placed in the darkest corner of the sleeping area. She will pull straw into it before kindling. Do not disturb the
kits
for the first 48 hours unless you suspect a dead kit that needs removing.
Keeping buck and doe separate
A
buck

kept in a colony run with does will re-mate the doe within hours of kindling and exhaust her with back-to-back pregnancies. Keep buck and doe in separate housing. Bring the doe to the buck for mating, then return her immediately. Never bring the buck to the doe's space; she will defend her territory aggressively.

items.fix: Check the roof felt and any joins for gaps. Raise the rear of the hutch slightly so it drains forward into the run. Change sleeping-area straw every 4 days.
items.cause: Poor drainage, leaking roof, or bedding not changed frequently enough
items.symptom: Wet floor in the sleeping area

items.fix: Reinforce that corner with doubled wire and extra staples. Check the entire perimeter and repair any loose sections. Add a single strand of electric fence at nose height outside the run.
items.cause: Fox or mink has been testing the wire during the night
items.symptom: Wire has been pushed inward at a corner

items.fix: Shade the hutch more effectively. Replace stale bedding. Check for mites or urine soaking into timber, which can irritate rabbits enough to keep them in the run.
items.cause: Too bright, too hot, or bedding not replaced recently
items.symptom: Rabbits refusing to use the sleeping compartment
